CITY OF OCEANSIDE

DEPUTY MAYOR
MELBA BISHOP

June 12, 1992


Wayne Ordos, Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Mr. Ordos:

I am writing to request an immunity letter from the Commission on
a potential conflict of interest pursuant to Government Code
§3114.

I have requested and received an opinion from Special Counsel D.
Dwight Worden which is attached for your review. The facts are as
stated in Mr. Worden's memorandum, and I understand that you will
assume these facts to be true.

I am requesting this since I want to make every effort to ensure
that I am in compliance with the law.

I will not participate in any votes relative to this project until
I receive your advice.

Sincerely,

Melba Bishop
Deputy Mayor

Enc.

MB/jlj


CIVIC CENTER • 300 NORTH HILL STREET • OCEANSIDE, CA 92054 • TELEPHONE 619-966-4404


FROM: DWIGHT WORDEN                TO: 6199664436      JUN 10, 1992   11:11AM    P.02

                    THE LAW OFFICES OF
            D. DWIGHT WORDEN
            A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION
                                                        D. DWIGHT WORDEN N
            749 LOMAS SANTA FE DRIVE, SUITE 102          TRACEY N. RICHMOND
            SOLANA BEACH, CA 92075-2076                  W. JAMES R. POTTER
                                                         JAMES G. ELLIS RI
            (619) 755-0004     (619) 755-0079            HENRY M. RIND
            FAX (619) 755-6198                           CRYSTAL CHAMI OHO


                                M E M O R A N D U M


TO:         Councilwoman Melba Bishop

FROM:       D. Dwight Worden, Special Counsel Re: MRF and Solid
            Waste Issues

DATE:       June 10, 1992

RE:         Materials Recovery Facility (MRF) and Potential
            Conflict of Interest


            As you know, my office was recently retained as Special
Counsel to the City of Oceanside to assist the City in its
negotiations concerning the potential location of a Materials
Recovery Facility (MRF) in the City. For some time my firm has
provided special counsel services to the City in the litigation
concerning the San Marcos Landfill. In this context, you have
asked my office for a legal opinion on a situation that has just
come to your attention which may raise conflict of interest
issues. You have expressed your intent to avoid not only the
reality of conflict, but also the appearance of conflict as well, and
have asked for an opinion and guidance.

            BASIC FACTS

            As I understand it, the basic facts giving rise to your
question can be summarized as follows:

            The City of Oceanside is interested in the possibility of
having a private waste hauling company build a private Materials
Recovery Facility somewhere within the City which can provide
recycling and other services to the City by contract. To pursue
this possibility, the City Council appointed you and
Councilmember Rodee as a Council subcommittee to oversee the
negotiations process and gave direction to City Staff to begin negotiations
for such a proposal. City direction is that the project, if one
is ultimately approved by the full Council, would be a private


DL/C110007.138

FROM:DWIGHT WORDEN                    TO:         6199664436          JUN 10, 1992  11:12AM  P.03


                                              Councilwoman Melba Bishop
                                              June 10, 1992
                                              Page 2

     project on private property built and operated by a private
company.  The city would receive services at the facility by
contract.

     City Staff has established a negotiating team and
preliminary meetings with the City's existing waste hauler have
been held.  The Staff has kept the Council subcommittee of Bishop
and Rodes informed of its progress.  Recently, my office was
retained to assist the City.  To date, no commentes have been
made, but the staff is in the process of working on a draft
Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with the private contractor
which it is hoped will be presented to the city Council for its
consideration in the near future.

     The private contractor has expressed a preliminary interest
in several potential sites in the City on which it would consider
building a MRF facility.  No commitments or decisions with
respect to siting have been made by the City, and none will be
made until full CEQA and permit review is completed.  City staff,
and the Council subcommittee, however, are aware of the potential
sites of interest to the private operator.

     It is in this context that you informed me that you just
learned (May 28, 1992) that one of the sites under consideration
by the private operator is subject to a private option in favor
of John Patterson.  You further advise that John Patterson has
provided legal services to members of your family in the past on
matters unrelated to city business, and has been a long time
personal friend and acquaintance of yours.

     It is in this context that you have asked for guidance as to
whether or not your service on the Council subcommittee and your
contact with the staff negotiating team creates a conflict
because of your personal relationship with John Patterson in
light of the newly discovered information that Mr. Patterson
holds an option on one of the potential sites.

     You further advise that you have no business or other
economic relations with Mr. Patterson, direct or indirect, and
you have no financial interest, direct or indirect, in any of the
sites.  And, you do not know any of the terms or details of the
Patterson option.

                         QUESTION PRESENTED

     In light of the foregoing facts, is a conflict of interest
presented because of Councilmember Bishop's personal relationship

DL/CITDOC7.138

FROM: DWIGHT WORDEN                TO:     6199664436          JUN 10, 1992    11:13AM  P.04


                                                      Councilwoman Melba Bishop
                                                      June 10, 1992
                                                      Page 3

     with John Patterson such that she should resign from the Council
     subcommittee appointed to oversee the MRF matter, and/or such
     that she should abstain from voting or participating in any of
     the decision making affecting the site in question?

                              CONCLUSION

     A.     The Political Reform Act.

          The Political Reform Act governs financial conflicts of
     interest of elected officials.  Since the facts are that
     Councilmember Bishop has no financial or economic interest,
     direct or indirect, within the meaning of the Act in the MRF or
     in any of the sites currently known to be under consideration,
     there is no conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act.
     The Political Reform Act does not require Councilmember Bishop to resign from
     the Council subcommittee or to abstain from voting or
     participating with respect to these matters.

          B.     Bias.

          Under Common Law principals of "bias" if Councilmember
     Bishop determines that her personal relationship with Mr. Patterson
     is such that she cannot be fair and impartial in carrying out her
     future duties as a member of the Council subcommittee and/or as a
     member of the City Council with respect to this project, then she
     should withdraw from the subcommittee and/or abstain from
     participating in any decision in which she cannot be impartial.
     If her personal relationship with Mr. Patterson does not
     compromise her ability to be fair and impartial there is no need
     to resign from the subcommittee or to abstain from participating
     in decisions.

          C.     Government Code Section 1090

          Government Code Section 1090 prohibits elected officials
     from having financial interests in contracts.  Since Councilwoman
     Bishop has no financial interest in any contract relating to the
     proposed MRF Government Code Section 1090 does not apply.

          D     Council Policy.

          City Council Policy 300-04 (copy attached) applies to
     Councilmember contacts with project applicants.  If and when Mr.
     Patterson becomes an applicant (he is not now an applicant)
     Councilmember Bishop should be careful to comply with this
     policy.

     DL/CITCOD7.138

FROM:DWIGHT WORDEN                TO:        6199664436        JUN 10, 1992    11:13AM    P.05


                                                          Councilwoman Melba Bishop
                                                          June 10, 1992
                                                          Page 4

                              DISCUSSION

     A.    The Political Reform Act.

          The Political Reform Act was enacted by the People of
     California as an initiative following the Watergate scandal of
     the early 1970's. Government Code section 81000 et seq. In
     short, a "conflict of interest" requiring that an elected
     official abstain from participating in or attempting to influence
     a decision, directly or indirectly, exists when:

               1.    The elected official is called upon to make a
          decision in which he or she has a financial interest
          within the meaning of the Act; and

               2.    It is reasonably foreseeable that the effect
          of that decision will have a material financial effect
          on the official's financial interest; and

               3.    The effect on the official's financial
          interest will be different from the effect on the
          government general public or a large group of persons. Government
          Code Section 87100; Admin Regs at 6 CCR Section 18700
          et seq.

          In this case the facts as related by Councilwoman Bishop are
     that she has no financial interest in any of the sites currently
     known to be under consideration for the MRF. Specifically, she
     reports no financial interest whatsoever in the specific site in
     which Mr. Patterson is reported to hold an option. She also
     reports that she has no business relationship with Mr. Patterson
     and no other economic ties with him, and that she is not a client
     and no other economic ties with him, and that she is not a client
     of his at the
     or otherwise financially involved with him in any way at the
     present time. She reports that Mr. Patterson did represent some
     of her family in the past on matters unrelated to City business.

          Assuming these facts to be true it is apparent that she does
     not have a "financial interest" in any decision she might be
     called upon to make regarding the MRF, either as a member of the
     City Council, as a member of the Council, and
     Council-appointed subcommittee or as a member of the Council, and
     that Mr. Patterson is not a "source of income" to her within the
     meaning of the Act. Since she has no "financial interest" at
     issue, she has no conflict of interest under the Political Reform
     Act.

          Because the first prong of the test for determining

DL/CITOC07.138

FROM: DWIGHT WORDEN                TO:         6199664436        JUN 10, 1992  11:14AM  P.06


                                                           Councilwoman Melba Bishop
                                                           June 10, 1992
                                                           Page 5

conflicts of interest under the Political Reform Act is not met,
it is unnecessary to analyze the other prong of the test.
It is noted, however, that should Ms. Bishop acquire a financial
interest in the future that could be affected, this situation
should be re-evaluated.

     B.    Common Law Bias.

     In addition to the statutory restrictions of the Political
Reform Act, the case law of California still in effect is to the
extent not preempted by statute.  The case law
recognizes certain rules on "Common Law Bias."  In general, this
common law bias doctrine holds that one individual cannot serve
two masters, and that elected officials owe an undivided duty of
loyalty to their public responsibilities.

     If an elected official has a "bias" because of a financial
or personal relationship with individuals who are affected by
official decisions, then the official should not participate in
any decision in which such bias precludes the official from being
fair and impartial in honoring his or her duty to the public
office.  See, Noble v. City of Palo Alto (1928) 89 Cal.App. 47,
51-52; People v. Watson (1971) 15 Cal.App.3d 28, 38-39; 58 Ops.
Cal. Atty. Gen. 670.

     The test in this situation is whether or not Councilmember
Bishop's personal relationship with Mr. Patterson is so strong as
to render her incapable of exercising her duties as a member of
the Council subcommittee in a
manner, honoring her duty of
fair, impartial, and objective manner, honoring her duty in
loyalty to the public.  If Councilmember Bishop determines, in
good conscience, that her relationship is such that she cannot be
totally fair and impartial because of Mr. Patterson's interests
and his relationship to her, she should resign from the
subcommittee and/or abstain from participating in any decision-
making where such bias exists.

     If, in good conscience, Councilmember Bishop determines that
she can continue to make fair and impartial decisions in spite of
the personal relationship, she may continue to serve on the
subcommittee and participate in all decision- making activities.

     I hope this information answers your questions. If you need
anything further, please let me know.

     To keep him apprised, I am providing a copy of this
memorandum to Joe Stine of your City Attorney's Office, and I

DL/C110CD7.138

FROM: DWIGHT WORDEN                TO:        6199664436          JUN 10, 1992    11:15AM    P.07


                                                    Councilwoman Melba Bishop
                                                    June 10, 1992
                                                    Page 6

     would suggest that a copy of this memorandum be placed in the
public record.

                                          Very truly yours,

                                          LAW OFFICES OF D. DWIGHT WORDEN
                                          A Professional Corporation


                                          D. Dwight Worden

     DDW:dl
     c:   Joe Stine


DL/CITQO7.138

sections.question:
In light of the foregoing facts, is a conflict of interest presented because of Councilmember Bishop's personal relationship

with John Patterson such that she should resign from the Council subcommittee appointed to oversee the MRF matter, and/or such that she should abstain from voting or participating in any of the decision making affecting the site in question?
sections.conclusion:
A. The Political Reform Act.

The Political Reform Act governs financial conflicts of interest of elected officials. Since the facts are that Councilmember Bishop has no financial or economic interest, direct or indirect, within the meaning of the Act in the MRF or in any of the sites currently known to be under consideration, there is no conflict of interest under the Act. The Political Reform Act does not require Councilmember Bishop to resign from the Council subcommittee or to abstain from voting or participating with respect to these matters.

B. Bias.

Under Common Law principals of "bias" if Councilmember Bishop determines that her personal relationship to Mr. Patterson is such that she cannot be fair and impartial in carrying out her future duties as a member of the Council subcommittee and/or as a member of the City Council with respect to this project, then she should withdraw from the subcommittee and/or abstain from participating in any decision in which she cannot be impartial. If her personal relationship with Mr. Patterson does not compromise her ability to be fair and impartial there is no need to resign from the subcommittee or to abstain from participating in decisions.

C. Government Code Section 1090

Government Code Section 1090 prohibits elected officials from having financial interests in contracts. Since Councilwoman Bishop has no financial interest in any contract relating to the proposed MRF Government Code Section 1090 does not apply.

D. Council Policy.

City Council Policy 300-04 (copy attached) applies to Councilmember contacts with project applicants. If and when Mr. Patterson becomes an applicant (he is not now an applicant) Councilmember Bishop should be careful to comply with this policy.
sections.analysis:
A. The Political Reform Act.

The Political Reform Act was enacted by the People of California as an initiative following the Watergate scandal of the early 1970's. Government Code section 81000 et seq. In short, a "conflict of interest" requiring that an elected official abstain from participating in or attempting to influence a decision, directly or indirectly, exists when:

1. The elected official is called upon to make a decision in which he or she has a financial interest within the meaning of the Act; and

2. It is reasonably foreseeable that the effect of that decision will have a material financial affect on the official's financial interest; and

3. The effect on the official's financial interest will be different from the effect on the general public or a large group of persons. Government Code Section 87100; Admin Regs at 6 CCR Section 18700 et seq.

In this case the facts as related by Councilwoman Bishop are that she has no financial interest in any of the sites currently known to be under consideration for the MRF. Specifically, she reports no financial interest whatsoever in the specific site in which Mr. Patterson is reported to hold an option. She also reports that she has no business relationship with Mr. Patterson and no other economic ties with him, and that she is not a client or otherwise financially involved with him in any way at the present time. She reports that Mr. Patterson did represent some of her family in the past on matters unrelated to City business.

Assuming these facts to be true it is apparent that she does not have a "financial interest" in any decision she might be called upon to make regarding the MRF, either as a member of the Council-appointed subcommittee or as a member of the Council, and that Mr. Patterson is not a "source of income" to her within the meaning of the Act. Since she has no "financial interest" at issue, she has no conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act.

Because the first prong of the test for determining

Councilwoman Melba Bishop  
June 10, 1992  

conflicts of interest under the Political Reform Act is not met, it is unnecessary to analyze the other two prongs of the test. It is noted, however, that should Ms. Bishop acquire a financial interest in the future that could be affected, this situation should be re-evaluated.

B. Common Law Bias.

In addition to the statutory restrictions of the Political Reform Act, the case law of California is still in effect to the extent not specifically preempted by statute. The case law recognizes certain rules on "Common Law Bias." In general, this Common Law Bias doctrine holds that one individual cannot serve two masters, and that elected officials owe an undivided duty of loyalty to their public responsibilities.

If an elected official has a "bias" because of a financial or personal relationship with individuals who are affected by official decisions, then the official should not participate in any decision in which such bias precludes the official from being fair and impartial in honoring his or her duty to the public office. See, Noble v. City of Palo Alto (1928) 89 Cal.App. 47, 51-52; People v. Watson (1971) 15 Cal.App.3d 28, 38-39; 58 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 670.

The test in this situation is whether or not Councilmember Bishop's personal relationship with Mr. Patterson is so strong as to render her incapable of exercising her duties as a member of the Council and as a member of the Council subcommittee in a fair, impartial, and objective manner, honoring her duty of loyalty to the public. If Councilmember Bishop determines, in good conscience, that her relationship is such that she cannot be totally fair and impartial because of Mr. Patterson's interests and his relationship to her, she should resign from the subcommittee and/or abstain from participating in any decision-making where such bias exists.

If, in good conscience, Councilmember Bishop determines that she can continue to make fair and impartial decisions in spite of the personal relationship, she may continue to serve on the subcommittee and participate in all decision-making activities.

I hope this information answers your questions. If you need anything further, please let me know.

To keep him apprised, I am providing a copy of this memorandum to Joe Stine of your City Attorney's Office, and I

Councilwoman Melba Bishop  
June 10, 1992  

would suggest that a copy of this memorandum be placed in the public record.

QUESTION: In light of the foregoing facts, is a conflict of interest presented because of Councilmember Bishop's personal relationship

with John Patterson such that she should resign from the Council subcommittee appointed to oversee the MRF matter, and/or such that she should abstain from voting or participating in any of the decision making affecting the site in question?

CONCLUSION: A. The Political Reform Act.

The Political Reform Act governs financial conflicts of interest of elected officials. Since the facts are that Councilmember Bishop has no financial or economic interest, direct or indirect, within the meaning of the Act in the MRF or in any of the sites currently known to be under consideration, there is no conflict of interest under the Act. The Political Reform Act does not require Councilmember Bishop to resign from the Council subcommittee or to abstain from voting or participating with respect to these matters.

B. Bias.

Under Common Law principals of "bias" if Councilmember Bishop determines that her personal relationship to Mr. Patterson is such that she cannot be fair and impartial in carrying out her future duties as a member of the Council subcommittee and/or as a member of the City Council with respect to this project, then she should withdraw from the subcommittee and/or abstain from participating in any decision in which she cannot be impartial. If her personal relationship with Mr. Patterson does not compromise her ability to be fair and impartial there is no need to resign from the subcommittee or to abstain from participating in decisions.

C. Government Code Section 1090

Government Code Section 1090 prohibits elected officials from having financial interests in contracts. Since Councilwoman Bishop has no financial interest in any contract relating to the proposed MRF Government Code Section 1090 does not apply.

D. Council Policy.

City Council Policy 300-04 (copy attached) applies to Councilmember contacts with project applicants. If and when Mr. Patterson becomes an applicant (he is not now an applicant) Councilmember Bishop should be careful to comply with this policy.
